aliases: 炸猪排盖饭 (Zhà Zhū Pái Gài Fàn)

chinese: 炸猪排盖饭 (Zhà Zhū Pái Gài Fàn)

food_subgroup: Rice bowl dish (donburi) — panko-crumbed pork cutlet and egg simmered in dashi-soy-mirin tare over steamed white rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Katsudon. FOODON_03315393 (rice dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Katsudon. A0CCG (rice dish with fried meat) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Katsudon or donburi rice bowl category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of katsudon weighs approximately 480g and provides around 715 kcal, 30g protein, 22g fat, and 82g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised; Egg, whole, cooked, fried; White rice, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Katsudon is a composite dish. Per-100g nutrient values were derived by component-weighted estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), pork loin cooked (FDC ID 174032), whole egg fried (FDC ID 173424), and onion cooked (FDC ID 170000), combined with standard panko oil-absorption factors (~11% oil uptake by weight). The input record's per-100g values (energy 149 kcal, protein 6.3g, fat 4.6g, carbohydrate 17.1g) are consistent with this USDA-derived composite estimate and are retained. Sodium value (219mg/100g = ~1,051mg per bowl) reflects dashi-soy-mirin tare contribution consistent with USDA soy sauce and mirin entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for katsudon as a composite dish. GI ~68 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 68 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The panko coating and mirin-soy tare contribute minor additional rapidly-digestible carbohydrates. GL per bowl = GI × (carbohydrate per serving in grams / 100) = 68 × (82 / 100) = 55.76, rounded to 55.8. This is classified as medium GI but very high GL due to the large rice portion (~56g of the 82g total carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium ~1,051mg per bowl (219mg/100g × 480g) — 53% of the 2,000mg hypertensive daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit katsudon to occasional consumption; the dashi-soy-mirin tare is fully integrated into the dish and cannot be separated, so sodium reduction is not possible at the table — if ordering as a set meal with miso soup, skip the miso soup to keep total meal sodium below 1,200mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load ~55.8 per bowl — one of the highest GL values among Japanese restaurant dishes; 82g total carbohydrate per bowl equals the full carbohydrate budget for lunch in a typical 1,600 kcal T2DM meal plan
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular katsudon consumption; if eaten, request a smaller rice portion ('少米'), eat protein and vegetables first to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and account for the full 82g carbohydrate load when planning the remainder of the day's intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.5g/100g (7.2g per bowl) from pork loin and frying oil, combined with high sodium (~1,051mg per bowl) and cholesterol (~384mg per bowl)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit katsudon to occasional consumption; the combination of deep-fried pork (saturated fat and cholesterol), high sodium from tare, and refined carbohydrate from white rice creates a multi-factor cardiovascular risk profile — choosing grilled fish or tofu-based donburi alternatives is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~55.8) promotes postprandial inflammation; however, pork loin provides high thiamine (B1 ~0.15mg/100g), zinc (~1mg/100g), and selenium (~14mcg/100g) which support immune function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume katsudon occasionally; the anti-inflammatory benefit of pork loin micronutrients (B1, zinc, selenium) is offset by the pro-inflammatory effect of the high GL and oxidised frying oils — frequency should be limited to once weekly or less.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Katsudon (カツ丼) is a classic Japanese comfort food rice bowl consisting of a panko-crumbed, deep-fried pork loin cutlet (tonkatsu) and thinly sliced onion simmered together in a sweet-savoury dashi-soy-mirin tare, bound with barely-set beaten egg, and served over a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The dish is a staple of Japanese home cooking and casual restaurant dining, widely available in Singapore at chains such as Tonkichi and Saboten. Per standard bowl (~480g): approximately 715 kcal, 30g protein, 22g fat (7.2g saturated), 82g carbohydrate, GI ~68, GL ~55.8, and ~1,051mg sodium. Katsudon has the second-highest glycemic load of all Japanese restaurant dishes in this database, after Japanese curry rice (GL ~59.5). The panko breadcrumb coating absorbs approximately 10–12% oil by weight during deep-frying, which is less than standard breadcrumbs. Pork loin is an exceptional source of thiamine (Vitamin B1, ~0.15mg/100g — among the highest of all common meats), which supports carbohydrate metabolism and nerve function. The egg component contributes Vitamin D (~0.5mcg/100g), Vitamin B12 (~0.4mcg/100g), and high-quality complete protein. Clinically, katsudon is unsuitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes (GL ~55.8, 82g carbohydrate per bowl) or hypertension (sodium ~1,051mg per bowl, 53% of hypertensive daily limit). The dish is dairy-free and contains gluten (panko), egg, soy, and wheat allergens.

confidence_reason: Katsudon is a composite dish. Per-100g nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (cooked white rice, pork loin cooked, whole egg fried, onion cooked) with standard panko oil-absorption factors applied. The input record values are consistent with this USDA-derived estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. No single approved-source entry for katsudon as a complete dish exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore; hence confidence is medium rather than high. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for this composite preparation are not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fraction data for katsudon as a composite dish are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite preparation is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy pork loin and egg entries — no industrial trans fats present). The input record cited Japan MEXT Standard Tables of Food Composition (8th Edition) as its source; this source is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Nutrient values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries and found to be consistent; USDA FoodData Central is cited as the approved source for this record. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for katsudon as a composite dish was found in an approved source. GL per bowl = 68 × (82 ÷ 100) = 55.76 ≈ 55.8. Sodium per bowl ~1,051mg (219mg/100g × 480g). Cholesterol per bowl ~384mg (80mg/100g × 480g). The dish is not halal-certified as standard preparation uses pork.
